title: Content governance: integration & alerting questionnaire
preview:
From: Jenna Alvarez <jenna@fincore.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rajiv Menon <r.menon@fincore.com>
Date: 2026-11-30T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Initial questionnaire: integration points for content governance

Hi Ben —

Thanks for taking this. As part of our procurement checklist I need a short, operational view of how Redwood integrates into our incident/alerting workflows for content safety incidents. The RFP team wants to see:

- How abuse/misuse alerts are generated (rule-based vs ML signal)
- Typical false-positive rates and how you measure them
- Notification/triage paths (PagerDuty/Slack/email) and support for customer-defined webhooks
- Evidence packaged with alerts (original prompt, model response, metadata)
- Escalation SLA expectations and logs/audit availability for audits

We plan to share this with our security operations and compliance teams. A concise operational summary + any sample incident report template would be helpful.

Appreciate it — Jenna
Jenna Alvarez
Senior Security Engineer, FinCore Financial
jenna@fincore.com


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jenna Alvarez <jenna@fincore.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-11-30T10:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Initial questionnaire: integration points for content governance

Jenna — thanks for the clear list. I pulled together an operational summary and attached a compact detection matrix and our alerting playbook. High level answers below; Monica added technical detail and an example incident template in a follow-up.

Summary (TL;DR):
- Signal generation: hybrid approach — deterministic policy checks for clearly prohibited content + ML-based classifiers and anomaly detectors for misuse patterns. We expose both raw signals and normalized severity scores.
- FP monitoring: we measure precision/recall per-signal on a rolling window (customer prompts sampled). Typical FP (precision) varies by model and workload; for content-safety-critical categories we aim for >92% precision on tuned classifiers, and we provide baseline metrics in the attached matrix.
- Notifications: native integrations with PagerDuty, Slack, webhook endpoints, and email. Customers can map severity -> routing and set escalation policies.
- Evidence: every alert bundles the triggering prompt, model response, model id/version, token-level deltas, top-K signal scores, and a short provenance block (region, serving node id, timestamp).
- SLAs & audit: Dedicated/Private customers can elect extended log retention and continuous audit streams (S3/GCS/HSM-signed logs). SLA-level alert-to-notification targets are configurable; we recommend 5–15m for high-severity routing.

Attachments: redwood_detection_matrix.xlsx (signal baselines), redwood_alerting-playbook.pdf (workflow + example incident report).

Next steps: Monica will reply with field-level schema for alerts (webhook payload), and Aditya can share contract language for log retention/SLA options. Happy to set a 30–45 minute walkthrough with your SOC team.

Best —
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.ai


From: Jenna Alvarez <jenna@fincore.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rajiv Menon <r.menon@fincore.com>
Date: 2026-11-30T11:20:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Initial questionnaire: integration points for content governance

Ben/Monica —

A few clarifying questions after skimming the matrix:
1) For webhook evidence payloads, can we request that original prompt text be redacted at our side and instead receive a SHA256 reference + access windowed link for forensic pulls? We need to keep PII minimization in place until an incident is escalated.
2) What drives the severity score? Is it a single classifier output or an ensemble? Any chance of surface-level examples (anonymized) that illustrate borderline cases?
3) For log retention: what encryption-at-rest and key management options exist for Private deployment vs Hosted?

If you can provide short answers to these 3 and the webhook schema, we can finalize the questionnaire and move to an internal review.

Thanks — Jenna


From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.ai>
To: Jenna Alvarez <jenna@fincore.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-01T08:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Initial questionnaire: integration points for content governance

Jenna — quick answers and a payload sketch below. I also attached an example incident record in the playbook.

1) Prompt redaction / hashed references:
- Yes. Our webhook payload supports either full_prompt or prompt_hash. For PII-sensitive customers we default to prompt_hash + time-limited presigned URL for retrieval (configurable TTL). Retrieval requests are logged and require an auth token bound to the customer org.

2) Severity computation:
- Ensemble approach: deterministic policy checks (binary) feed into a higher-level scoring model along with classifier confidence, conversational context (recent prompt history), and behavioral signals (rate, repeated prompts). The final severity is a normalized score (0-100) and a categorical label (LOW/MED/HIGH/CRITICAL).
- Borderline examples: included in redwood_detection_matrix.xlsx under the "Notes: borderline" tab (anonymized). We also offer per-customer tuning to shift thresholds for model-specific patterns.

3) Encryption & KMS:
- Hosted: data encrypted at rest with customer-managed keys (bring-your-own-key via AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or Azure Key Vault) for enterprise plans.
- Dedicated & Private: full customer KMS/HSM integration, option for on-prem HSM for signing logs. All logs can be streamed to customer-owned buckets with server-side encryption.

Webhook schema (condensed):
- alert_id (string)
- timestamp (ISO8601)
- severity_score (int 0-100)
- severity_label (string)
- triggering_signal_ids (list)
- prompt_hash (string) or prompt_text (string)
- response_excerpt (string) — truncated to N chars by policy
- model_id, model_version
- provenance: region, serving_node (string)
- presigned_retrieval_url (optional)
- evidence_attachment_keys (list) -> keys in customer S3/GCS path

We can provide the full JSON schema in the Drive folder if you want it in machine-readable form.

Let me know if you want field-level examples; I can paste an anonymized sample in the thread.

—Monica
Monica Patel
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
monica_patel@redwood.ai


From: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
To: Jenna Alvarez <jenna@fincore.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-01T13:10:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Initial questionnaire: integration points for content governance

Jenna — one operational note on SLAs and contracting:
- We can include a 15-minute alert-routing objective for CRITICAL severity in Dedicated plans (contractually defined). For Hosted customers we commit to notification within 30 minutes for CRITICAL, with a 99.9% uptime SLA for notification endpoints.
- Log retention tiers available: 30/90/365+ days; longer retention available as an add-on with egress pricing.
- Contract language: we have standard clauses for evidentiary access and audit cooperation; I linked a sample clause in the HubSpot deal record (link below).

If you confirm the desired retention and whether you prefer prompt_hash-only by default, I can kick off a tailored SOW and send an initial DocuSign with the retention addendum.

Cheers —
Aditya Rao
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
aditya_rao@redwood.ai


From: Jenna Alvarez <jenna@fincore.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rajiv Menon <r.menon@fincore.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-02T16:34:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Initial questionnaire: integration points for content governance

Thanks — this is exactly what we needed. Confirming FinCore preferences: 
- Default to prompt_hash + presigned retrieval for all alerts (TTL 48 hours).
- Retention: 365 days for incident logs, with streaming to our S3 bucket (we will provide bucket + KMS).
- Please add the CRITICAL 15-minute routing objective into the SOW for Dedicated.

Next steps per our internal calendar:
- Ben: schedule a 45-minute walkthrough for the SOC team (we prefer 12/07 at 10:00am PT if available).
- Aditya: please prepare a SOW draft + retention addendum and send DocuSign for review.
- Monica: post the full webhook JSON schema and an anonymized incident example to the Drive playbook folder.

> 2) Severity computation:

The final severity is a normalized score (0-100) and a categorical label (LOW/MED/HIGH/CRITICAL).
>

title: RFP: Data Locality + Conditional Edge Failover — Audit Evidence Matrix
preview:
From: Anna Müller <procurements@paneuropa-health.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example>
Cc: Marco Rossi <security@paneuropa-health.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 2026 17:12:00 +0000
Subject: PEH RFP Section 3 — conditional failover & egress logging clarifications

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the initial packet. Section 3 of our RFP digs into two things we consider gating: (1) conditional edge failover where requests originating in EU primary sites can temporarily route to non-EU capacity when capacity/latency thresholds are breached and (2) egress and audit trail evidence for all cross-border events.

Can you confirm: 
- Whether Redwood supports configurable hard vs. soft routing policies per account (i.e., hard prohibit cross-border vs. soft-fallback with audit and masking)?
- Retention policy for egress audit logs and whether those logs can be exported to our S3/GCS in-country?
- Whether the KV/prefix caches ever persist outside the primary region during a failover and, if so, how you protect PHI in that scenario (encryption, key separation, deletion guarantees)?

Attached is our template (Section3_Attach.pdf) — we attempted to mark the high-risk rows. Please provide a response matrix and any evidence artifacts (SOC2 pages, cross-border data flow diagrams) by COB Wednesday so our technical reviewers can validate.

Best,
Anna
Procurement Manager
PanEuropa Health Systems

Attachment: Section3_Attach.pdf (PDF stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example>
To: Anna Müller <procurements@paneuropa-health.com>
Cc: Marco Rossi <security@paneuropa-health.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.example>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.example>
Date: Tue, 01 Dec 2026 08:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: PEH RFP Section 3 — conditional failover & egress logging clarifications

Anna, Marco —

Thanks for the callout and the marked up template. Attached is a focused response matrix (Edge-Failover-Matrix.xlsx) plus two artifacts: an extract from our SOC2 audit relevant to logging, and a short architecture diagram showing our regional control plane and routing tiers (PEH_RFP_Section3_DataResidency.pdf). Key summary below so your team can triage quickly: 

- Routing policy model: Redwood supports per-account routing policies with three pre-built modes: HARD (block cross-border; return 403/429), SOFT (allow fallback to compatible model variants in other regions when latency or capacity SLO is breached), and HYBRID (soft fallback plus enforced PII masking and chained audit events). These can be configured via account policy or per-route override.

- Egress & audit logs: All cross-border routing events emit structured audit events (timestamp, source_region, destination_region, request_id, model_variant, token_count). By default audit logs are retained 90 days in our control plane; we support configurable retention to your minimum (including 365/730 days) and can stream a copy to your customer-owned S3/GCS bucket in EU region via signed, time-limited transfers. Audit exports are delivered as compressed newline-delimited JSON with accompanying schema.

- Cache residency: Prefix and KV caches are region-bound under HARD and HYBRID modes. Under SOFT fallback, ephemeral cache shards may be materialized in fallback region for performance; these shards are encrypted with regional KMS keys and marked for expedited purge on session termination. Our policy: ephemeral shards are zeroed and metadata purged within 2 hours post-session; persistent caches are never written to secondary regions unless explicitly opted-in.

Attached: Edge-Failover-Matrix.xlsx, PEH_RFP_Section3_DataResidency.pdf, SOC2-Audit-Redwood-2026.zip

Next steps: Jonas and Karthik are looped for technical detail. Would you like a 45-minute technical deep dive this Thursday to walk the matrix row-by-row?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.example
Mobile: +1 (415) 555-0182

Attachments: Edge-Failover-Matrix.xlsx (Excel stub), PEH_RFP_Section3_DataResidency.pdf (PDF), SOC2-Audit-Redwood-2026.zip (ZIP stub)

From: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.example>
To: Anna Müller <procurements@paneuropa-health.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.example>, Marco Rossi <security@paneuropa-health.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 10:20:00 +0100
Subject: Re: PEH RFP Section 3 — conditional failover & egress logging clarifications

Anna/Team —

Adding a few technical clarifications to Ben's summary that your SREs will likely probe: 

- KMS/keys: Customer-managed keys are supported for both at-rest model weights and for caching encryption. For Private deployments we recommend dual-key separation (customer KMS for data + Redwood-managed key for control-plane metadata). Cross-region key wrapping is not performed unless explicitly configured; we never export plaintext keys.

- Evidence & reproducibility: We can provide an extract of routing decisions (signed) for a given request_id, including the policy version and the kernel selection that executed. Those signed traces are useful for audits; we generate them on request for a given date range and can deliver checksums and a chain-of-custody statement.

- Data deletion SLA: For account-level deletion we commit to 30-day purge for user content and model request logs (configurable to 7 days for stricter customers), and 2-hour purge for ephemeral cache shards after session termination under SOFT. We provide a bulk deletion API and an offline attestation (hash-based) on completion.

- Masking: HYBRID mode supports automated token-level masking rules before fallback transfer; these are configurable regex-based rules plus an optional ML-based PII detector. Masked payloads still generate audit events showing a masking flag rather than raw content.

Happy to join the technical call. Propose Thu 2026-12-03 10:00 CET (09:00 UK) / 01:00 PST.

- Jonas
Principal Architect, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

(On Tue, 01 Dec 2026 at 08:03 -0800, Ben Carter wrote:)
> Anna, Marco —
>
> Thanks for the callout and the marked up template. Attached is a focused response matrix...
>
> Ben


From: Marco Rossi <security@paneuropa-health.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.example>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.example>
Date: Thu, 02 Dec 2026 15:41:00 +0000
Subject: Re: PEH RFP Section 3 — conditional failover & egress logging clarifications

Ben/Jonas/Karthik —

Appreciate the rapid, detailed replies. The matrix looks good at a high level. A couple of follow-ups from security: 

1) For the signed routing traces — can you add an HSM-backed timestamped signature on the trace export? We need non-repudiation for audit.
2) For streaming audit exports to our EU S3, we need proof-of-delivery (MD5/SHA256) and an option for server-side encryption with a customer-provided KMS key.

If those two items are supported as described, we'll move this section to green and schedule the procurement review.

Confirming the technical deep dive on Thu 2026-12-03 10:00 CET — Marco (security), Anna (procurement) + our infra team will attend. Ben — please send a calendar invite and a temporary read-only link to the Drive folder with the attachments you shared.

Thanks,
Marco Rossi
Head of Security — PanEuropa Health Systems

Anna, Marco —

Thanks for the callout and the marked up template. Attached is a focused response matrix (Edge-Failover-Matrix.xlsx) plus two artifacts: an extract from our SOC2 audit relevant to logging, and a short architecture diagram showing our regional control plane and routing tiers (PEH_RFP_Section3_DataResidency.pdf). Key summary below so your team can triage quickly: 

- Routing policy model: Redwood supports per-account routing policies with three pre-built modes: HARD (block cross-border; return 403/429), SOFT (allow fallback to compatible model variants in other regions when latency or capacity SLO is breached), and HYBRID (soft fallback plus enforced PII masking and chained audit events). These can be configured via account policy or per-route override.

- Egress & audit logs: All cross-border routing events emit structured audit events (timestamp, source_region, destination_region, request_id, model_variant, token_count). By default audit logs are retained 90 days in our control plane; we support configurable retention to your minimum (including 365/730 days) and can stream a copy to your customer-owned S3/GCS bucket in EU region via signed, time-limited transfers. Audit exports are delivered as compressed newline-delimited JSON with accompanying schema.

- Cache residency: Prefix and KV caches are region-bound under HARD and HYBRID modes. Under SOFT fallback, ephemeral cache shards may be materialized in fallback region for performance; these shards are encrypted with regional KMS keys and marked for expedited purge on session termination. Our policy: ephemeral shards are zeroed and metadata purged within 2 hours post-session; persistent caches are never written to secondary regions unless explicitly opted-in.

Attached: Edge-Failover-Matrix.xlsx, PEH_RFP_Section3_DataResidency.pdf, SOC2-Audit-Redwood-2026.zip

Next steps: Jonas and Karthik are looped for technical detail. Would you like a 45-minute technical deep dive this Thursday to walk the matrix row-by-row?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.example
Mobile: +1 (415) 555-0182

Attachments: Edge-Failover-Matrix.xlsx (Excel stub), PEH_RFP_Section3_DataResidency.pdf (PDF), SOC2-Audit-Redwood-2026.zip (ZIP stub)

From: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.example>
To: Anna Müller <procurements@paneuropa-health.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.example>, Marco Rossi <security@paneuropa-health.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 10:20:00 +0100
Subject: Re: PEH RFP Section 3 — conditional failover & egress logging clarifications

Anna/Team —

Adding a few technical clarifications to Ben's summary that your SREs will likely probe: 

- KMS/keys: Customer-managed keys are supported for both at-rest model weights and for caching encryption. For Private deployments we recommend dual-key separation (customer KMS for data + Redwood-managed key for control-plane metadata). Cross-region key wrapping is not performed unless explicitly configured; we never export plaintext keys.

- Evidence & reproducibility: We can provide an extract of routing decisions (signed) for a given request_id, including the policy version and the kernel selection that executed. Those signed traces are useful for audits; we generate them on request for a given date range and can deliver checksums and a chain-of-custody statement.

- Data deletion SLA: For account-level deletion we commit to 30-day purge for user content and model request logs (configurable to 7 days for stricter customers), and 2-hour purge for ephemeral cache shards after session termination under SOFT. We provide a bulk deletion API and an offline attestation (hash-based) on completion.

- Masking: HYBRID mode supports automated token-level masking rules before fallback transfer; these are configurable regex-based rules plus an optional ML-based PII detector. Masked payloads still generate audit events showing a masking flag rather than raw content.

Happy to join the technical call. Propose Thu 2026-12-03 10:00 CET (09:00 UK) / 01:00 PST.

- Jonas
Principal Architect, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

> Thanks for the callout and the marked up template. Attached is a focused response matrix...

Appreciate the rapid, detailed replies. The matrix looks good at a high level. A couple of follow-ups from security: 

1) For the signed routing traces — can you add an HSM-backed timestamped signature on the trace export? We need non-repudiation for audit.
2) For streaming audit exports to our EU S3, we need proof-of-delivery (MD5/SHA256) and an option for server-side encryption with a customer-provided KMS key.

If those two items are supported as described, we'll move this section to green and schedule the procurement review.

title: Competitive trial top-up request - ClearSight bakeoff
preview:

Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>

Subject: Redwood trial top-up for ClearSight bakeoff

Hi Sara —

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. We appreciate ClearSight including Redwood in the evaluation. You mentioned needing additional GPU-backed token budget to run the larger batch of classification and NER tests your team uses to stress latency and throughput.

We can provision an additional 200k token eval credit (GPU-optimized) and extend the trial window by two weeks to cover your scheduled benchmark runs. Before I commit this, a couple of quick asks from our side:

- Can you confirm the start date for your final run (so we align the window)?
- Will the trial be run by a single engineering account or multiple users (affects quota and rate limits)?
- Are there any special data retention or IP terms we should be aware of?

If you confirm the run date and single account usage, I’ll get this approved and provisioned today. I’m attaching a short summary of what the top-up includes and a draft SOW for procurement (attachment: SOW-ClearSight-draft.pdf, trial-credit-summary.xlsx).

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com

Subject: Re: Redwood trial top-up for ClearSight bakeoff

Ben —

Appreciate the quick follow-up. Final run is scheduled for 2026-12-07 (internal QA on 12/04). We plan to run the suite from a single engineering service account (ci-bakeoff@clearsight.ai). No special data/IP terms — all data is synthetic/anonymized for the benchmark.

Subject: Fwd: Redwood trial top-up for ClearSight bakeoff — approval request

Karthik — quick note. ClearSight is running a competitive bake-off next week (final run 12/07). They asked for an extra 200k token GPU-backed eval credit and a two-week extension. Procurement wants a PO-friendly SOW line item.

Why I’m asking: ClearSight is actively evaluating three vendors; our latency profile in the last perf test looked favorable but the experiment needs to scale to the larger dataset (the one that exposed batching and KV cache behavior). Approving this top-up improves our chance to be chosen and is low-cost compared to a pilot contract.

Proposed terms:
- 200,000 token credit, expires 2027-01-07
- Provisioned to account ci-bakeoff@clearsight.ai with token/throughput caps (I propose 120 rps max)
- PO/SOW line: "Evaluation credits: GPU-backed inference credits for ClearSight Labs bakeoff (200k tokens) — no monetary charge; issued as product credit."

Attachments: SOW-ClearSight-draft.pdf (attached) and trial-credit-summary.xlsx.

Can you approve or suggest edits? Monica — please flag any finance/ops gotchas.

Thanks,
Ben



From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Sara Taylor <sara.taylor@clearsight.ai>
Date: 2026-12-02T14:35:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Redwood trial top-up for ClearSight bakeoff — approval request

Ben / Monica — approved with the conditions below. Sara — looping you in so procurement has clarity.

Approval: Provide 200k GPU-backed token credits, provisioned to ci-bakeoff@clearsight.ai, expiration 2027-01-07. Please apply the following controls:
1) Hard throughput cap: 120 rps (enforced at the account level).
2) Logging: enable a short retention export (14 days) for us to capture perf traces — no content retention beyond the trial window.
3) Track: issue a promo code/reference (promo_ref: CSL-BB-47-TOPUP) and attach the HubSpot deal link so we can tie consumption to the opportunity.

- Identity: We support SAML2.0 SSO (IdP metadata or manual cert + login URL), SCIM2.0 provisioning (tokened endpoints + documented attribute mapping), and a role-based admin model with explicit admin vs. operator scopes.

- Ben Carter (cc'd) will provide the SAML metadata sample, SCIM endpoint docs, and our RBAC matrix export.

3) Legal: feel free to use the attached SOC summary; we'll follow with a BAA if needed.

I'm attaching the SOC summary and a sample SSO metadata file to get you started. If helpful we can do a 20-min technical sync with your infra team — propose 2026-12-03 10:00 AM PST (open to alternative times).

Thanks — Marissa

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>

Subject: Fwd: Data governance — training + retention clarification

Team — forwarding Aurora's questions. They need formal contract language and a deletion workflow by EOW; procurement will not move without it. Key asks:
- Explicit "no training" guarantee text we can commit to in the enterprise T&C.
- Default retention windows and options for shorter retention or customer-managed logs.
- Deletion steps and timeframe for on-demand erasure requests (including confirmation).

Ava — can you prepare a one-page technical appendix that lists where request/response logs live, how KV cache is retained (if at all for hosted vs dedicated), and deletion mechanics?
Grace — can you draft the legal clause (two options: standard and more prescriptive) and confirm any exceptions (e.g., aggregated telemetry, anonymized metrics)?

From: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 01 Dec 2026 15:20:37 -0800
Subject: RE: Fwd: Data governance — training + retention clarification

Ben / team — draft language and implementation notes below. I've kept it concise so it can be copy/pasted into Aurora's PO or SOW. Please review and flag any technical inaccuracies.

Legal assurance (recommended clause - non-training):
"Redwood Inference shall not use, access, or incorporate Customer Data for the purpose of training, improving, or fine-tuning any machine learning models, model weights, or third-party model artifacts, except where Customer provides explicit, written opt-in for a defined program. This restriction applies to all model training pipelines, both internal and third-party, and includes any derived representations of Customer Data. Aggregated telemetry that cannot be associated with Customer Data and that is used solely to maintain service reliability is excluded from this prohibition."

Retention & deletion summary (technical + process):
- Hosted API (default): request/response logs stored for 30 days for debugging and fraud detection; billing metering retained for 90 days.
- Dedicated (reserved) and Private (VPC/on-prem): customers can configure retention via policy; we can support retention windows down to 0 days for ephemeral logging in Dedicated clusters.
- KV cache: ephemeral in-memory cache by default for inference sessions; not persisted to long-term storage for hosted non-training accounts. For Dedicated/private deployments, KV persistence is configurable per-customer.
- Deletion workflow: customers submit an erasure request via support portal or API; standard SLA: initial acknowledgement within 24 hours, completion within 5 business days for hosted accounts (shorter windows negotiable in contract). We provide signed confirmation with transaction id.

Attachments provided: training_assurance_clauses.docx (legal text variants), SOC2_summary_slide.png (controls summary).

Notes / exceptions:
- We may retain anonymized, aggregated telemetry to monitor system health and SLOs; such telemetry will not contain Customer Data.
- For customers who opt into a model improvement program, we require an explicit separate agreement with scoped data handling terms.

If this looks good I can send to Samira directly with the attachments.

Gracey
Grace O'Connor
Director, Legal & Compliance, Redwood Inference
grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com

title: CAIQ: residency attestation and short runtime-sample for marketplace listing
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sanjay Patel <sanjay@neptunehealth.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: CAIQ: residency attestation and short runtime-sample for marketplace listing

Sanjay —

Thanks again for the quick sync this morning. As discussed, Neptune's marketplace listing process requires a filled CAIQ plus two supporting artifacts: a short runtime log sample showing token residency (date-limited) and a brief attestation that logging/retention practises meet the marketplace residency requirements.

High-level ask: please confirm whether a 7‑day runtime snapshot (UTC 2026‑11‑08 to 2026‑11‑15) and a signed attestation will satisfy your intake checklist. If the team needs a different window or additional metadata fields (tenant id obfuscation, sequence ID, request path), let me know.

Proposed timeline:
- We produce the runtime sample and attestation draft by EOD 2026‑12‑02
- Neptune does a short technical review and confirms acceptance by 2026‑12‑05

I’m looping in Jordan (marketplace lead) and Marissa (CS) to track the intake and handoff to compliance.

Thanks —
Aditya
Head of Sales Ops
Redwood Inference

Attachments: (none in this message)

From: Sanjay Patel <sanjay@neptunehealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T11:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: CAIQ: residency attestation and short runtime-sample for marketplace listing

Aditya —

Thanks for the clarity. The intake team is okay with a 7‑day window; two quick clarifications before we proceed:

1) Redaction: we require tenant identifiers to be pseudonymized (sha256-token or similar) in the sample.
2) Scope: include only request/response latency headers and residency tag (region code). No payloads.

If you can provide the snapshot in a zipped CSV with the columns: pseudonymized_tenant_id, timestamp_utc, region_code, request_path_stub, token_count, latency_ms that should be perfect.

Also please include a one‑paragraph attestation signed by Redwood legal or CISO that logs were captured and stored in the APAC region for the date range provided. A short DocuSign link is fine for signature.

If you can hit the 2026‑12‑02 timeline, I’ll escalate to the marketplace intake for faster review.

Regards,
Sanjay
Neptune Health — Marketplace Intake

Aditya

title: Data fencing and control-plane pathways — next steps for MedLinc POC
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Tom Rivers <tom.rivers@medlinc.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 1 Dec 2026 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call recap — data fencing & control-plane pathways

Tom — great conversation this morning. Quick recap and a short set of asks so we can scope the POC accurately.

What we covered (high-level)
- MedLinc priority: strict separation of customer data from other tenants and minimal Redwood-managed control-plane exposure.
- Options discussed: Hosted API (fastest), Dedicated capacity (reserved GPU pools + tenant isolation), Private VPC/on-prem (full control-plane residency).
- Primary concerns: audit logs & retention, key management (BYOK/HSM), network egress rules, evidence for SOC 2 controls and data-plane custody.

Clarifying questions / asks (please reply inline)
1) For the data you want fenced — is it PHI or regulated PII, or a mix? (simple: PHI / PII / non-sensitive)
2) Do you require the control plane to be customer-managed (Private) or is a Redwood-hosted control plane acceptable if the data plane is entirely isolated in your VPC?
3) Any mandatory encryption requirements (FIPS 140-2 / HSM-backed keys / BYOK)?
4) Who should I loop from procurement for contract language and any custom SLA/penalty terms? (name + email)
5) Expected throughput for the POC (rps / avg tokens per request) and preferred model family (LlamaX / Red-Base / etc.)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Tom Rivers <tom.rivers@medlinc.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 1 Dec 2026 14:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap — technical notes on isolation for MedLinc

Tom / Ben — quick technical notes to surface tradeoffs and how we typically implement each option. I’ll keep this practical.

1) Hosted API
- Control plane and orchestration: Redwood-managed in our cloud.
- Data plane: request payloads transit Redwood-managed endpoints; we offer customer-encrypted-at-rest storage but the network path traverses Redwood infrastructure.
- Use case: rapid integration, not recommended for PHI in production.

2) Dedicated capacity (Reserved GPU pools)
- Data plane residency: we can dedicate GPU hosts and tenant namespaces so your inference workloads do not share model weights or runtime processes with other customers.
- Cache behavior: prefix & KV caches are able to be configured per-tenant; in Dedicated mode caches live on the same region-facing hosts we manage. We can offer an option to encrypt cache segments with tenant keys. However control-plane APIs (rollouts, autoscaling signals) remain Redwood-hosted.
- Keys: BYOK available for storage and cache encryption; HSM-backed keys can be configured for production with additional compliance steps.

3) Private VPC (customer VPC + private control plane)
- Full control-plane residency: we deploy Redwood control-plane components into your VPC (or on-prem) and hand over a customer-managed endpoint.
- Network & egress: all traffic can be restricted by security groups, no outbound to public internet, and we can provide sample ACLs and flow logs.
- Audit logs & access: access to audit trails is local to your environment; Redwood provides a log-forwarder and schema so you get the same telemetry but stored under your retention policy.

Key operational notes
- KV cache eviction: for strict custody you can opt to disable persistent KV caching across requests; ephemeral in-memory cache remains at runtime.
- Telemetry: we can separate telemetry by tenant and redact content fields if requested.
- Evidence: SOC2 summary included; for HSM attestations we’ll need a security questionnaire + slightly longer lead time.

Next steps (technical)
- I propose a 60m network walk-through to map flows (we’ll bring an infra engineer).
- I’ll attach a control-plane responsibilities matrix (redwood-controlplane-matrix.csv) and a short Dedicated vs Private side-by-side that calls out where caches live and how keys are handled.

--

title: Operational controls for regional availability and export-control exceptions
preview:
From: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@medsys-eu.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-19T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Questions on EU-only processing, transient caches, and export-control screening

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the product briefing — the MedSys procurement team has a handful of follow-ups specific to our EU deployments. We appreciate concise confirmations we can hand to Legal and Infrastructure. High-level asks below (attached RFI PDF with numbered sections):

1) Can you confirm that inference for EU customers can be guaranteed to remain in EU regions (no cross-border token or KV cache replication) for production workloads? If there are short-lived exceptions (e.g., warm-up traffic), please define maximum windows.

2) We use a mix of sensitive patient metadata and non-identifying prompts. Do you offer configurable transient cache TTLs (and can they be set to zero) so ephemeral state never persists beyond N minutes?

3) What is Redwood's stance on export-control screening for model artifacts and tooling? For some regulated workflows we cannot allow model weights or derivative artifacts to transit non-EU jurisdictions for review.

4) If a regional outage triggers a fallback to another region, what operational mitigations are in place to avoid data egress (pseudonymization, encrypted staging, manual approval)?

We are compiling answers for Legal; Marissa will coordinate follow-ups. Can you please reply with short bullet answers we can include in our RFP response?

Thanks,
Elena

Attachments: MedSys_RFI_questions_20261119.pdf


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@medsys-eu.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-20T08:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Questions on EU-only processing, transient caches, and export-control screening

Elena — thanks for the clear list. Short answers below; Jonas and Laura are copied for technical and security detail. We'll follow up with a short annex for Legal.

1) EU-only inference: Yes — for Dedicated and Private deployments customers can restrict serving to EU regions only. For Hosted (multi-tenant) we provide region pinning and routing policies; default behavior is region-of-origin routing, but in rare automated failover the request may route to a compatible EU mirror or (only with explicit consent) to a pre-approved non-EU fallback. Max warm-up exceptions: 15 minutes by default; can be set to 0 for strict customers.

2) Transient cache TTLs: We support configurable TTLs including immediate cache bypass (TTL=0). Ephemeral KV cache persists in-region only and is encrypted at-rest. Customers can opt into no-cache mode for particular routes.

3) Export-control screening: Redwood performs an export-control classification on models we distribute; for customer-supplied models we can commit to not moving model artifacts out of region. For hosted onboarding workflows that require deeper analysis, we offer an escrowed air-gapped review process for Legal/Compliance review (requires Private or Dedicated engagement).

4) Fallback mitigation: Default automated fallback preserves data residency by preferring other EU capacity. If forced failover to non-EU is ever triggered, we have a runbook: (a) pseudonymize payloads client-side (recommended) or server-side with customer-managed KMS; (b) encrypt payloads with per-region key-wrapping; (c) notify customer + require explicit manual approval for persistent cross-border processing.

Next steps: Jonas will add a short technical appendix for your infra team; Laura will attach suggested contract language (SCC/DPA addendum). I'll prepare a hosting annex and a draft SLA clause for regional availability.

Best,
Ben

Attachments: redwood_regional_availability_digest_20261120.pdf


From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@medsys-eu.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-23T10:04:00-08:00
Subject: Technical appendix — ephemeral cache, model staging, and failover mechanics

Elena — quick technical notes you can share with Infra: feel free to pull into your RFP appendix.

- KV/prefix cache behavior: KV caches are sharded by tenant and region. There is no cross-region replication of live KV entries. Cache write-through to long-term storage only occurs when customer-configured persistence is enabled; default is memory-only and purged on restart or TTL expiry.

- Cache TTL and control plane: TTLs are set in routing rules per-route. For Hosted customers, the default max TTL is 24 hours; we support per-route overrides down to 0. For Dedicated/Private we can harden the control plane so only customer admins can change TTLs.

- Model artifact handling: Model uploads for Private/Dedicated are stored in-region. For Hosted model onboarding we offer two paths: (a) lightweight metadata-only compatibility tests run in-region; (b) deep artifact validation (optional) in an air-gapped sandbox — this is performed in-region for EU customers if requested.

- Failover flows: 3 modes — "regional-only" (no cross-border): reject or queue requests if capacity unavailable; "manual-approval" (notify and wait); "auto-fallback" (route to pre-approved fallback). We recommend "manual-approval" for regulated workloads.

- Observability & audit: Every routing decision is logged with region, node-id, and decision-reason. Logs can be exported via syslog/S3 to EU storage.

If you want, we can include a small diagram for your RFP.

On Fri, Nov 20, 2026 at 08:45 AM -0800, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Elena — thanks for the clear list. Short answers below; Jonas and Laura are copied for technical and security detail. We'll follow up with a short annex for Legal.


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-30T09:30:00-08:00
Subject: Security & contractual controls: SCCs, KMS, and export-control handling

Elena — notes from Security/Legal perspective you can share with MedSys Legal. Happy to jump on a call to walk through.

- SCC / DPA: Redwood supports EU Standard Contractual Clauses and offers a DPA with region binding clauses available as an annex. We can add explicit language restricting data processing to EU regions and requiring prior written consent for any exception. (See attached SCC-addendum.docx)

- Key management & key-wrapping: For Dedicated/Private we support customer-managed keys (BYOK) in EU KMS; for Hosted we provide per-tenant keys with HSM-backed wrapping and region-bound key material. Cross-region key export is disabled unless explicitly enabled by the customer.

- Export-control screening and model artifacts: For model artifacts, we will not transfer weights or derivatives out of EU without customer consent. For internal model analysis that needs human review, we offer an audited, in-region review queue or an air-gapped review on Dedicated/Private.

- Provenance & audit logs: Access to routing logs, node-level provenance, and any temporary staging records can be exported to EU-only storage for up to the retention period required by contract. We also provide an audit trail for any manual approvals that cause cross-border processing.

- Contractual mitigations for failover: We can include a clause that requires Redwood to attempt EU capacity for a configurable window and that any non-EU fallback requires a manual approval step and is logged with business justification.

Please advise if MedSys Legal wants redlines on the SCC-addendum.

Regards,
Laura Bennett
Director, Security Compliance
Redwood Inference

Attachments: SCC-addendum.docx; kms-ops-runbook.pdf


From: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@medsys-eu.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T16:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Operational controls for regional availability and export-control exceptions

Ben/Laura/Jonas — this is very helpful, thank you. MedSys Legal would like the SCC-addendum redline and the hosting annex you mentioned. Two asks before we close the RFP: 1) please provide the specific contractual text we can paste into our vendor agreement that enforces manual-approval for any non-EU fallback; 2) provide a one-page runbook summary that MedSys SREs can follow during a regional capacity event (we need phone + pager contact, expected timing for manual approval, and exact API calls to toggle manual-approval mode).

Marissa will coordinate the doc exchange and a short call next week.

Best,
Elena

title: Data residency & retention: hosted API — Q&A
preview:
From: Gina Park <gina@acmetech.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 29 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Hosted API — residency & retention clarifications

Hi Aisha / Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week. Our security team is working through the vendor questionnaire and had a couple of targeted asks about Redwood's hosted API:

1) If we use the hosted API, can we ensure all customer data (requests & generated content) remains within US regions only?
2) What is the default retention window for request/response logs and model telemetry? Where can we find a formal purge process and SLA?
3) For compliance, they also want to know whether backups/snapshots might replicate data outside the requested region and how long those are retained.

Can you point me to a short, plain-language summary and any attachments we can pass to InfoSec? We're on a tight timeline for our vendor review.

Appreciate it —
Gina
Security & Privacy, Acme Tech

---


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Gina Park <gina@acmetech.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi_feldman@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 2026 10:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API — residency & retention clarifications

Hi Gina —

Thanks — great questions. Short answers up front, followed by a few operational details and attachments. I've CC'd Marcus (security) and Naomi (product ops) for technical backup.

1) Regional residency: Yes — when provisioning a hosted API key/account you can specify a US-only serving region (us-west-2 or us-east-1). All runtime request processing, ephemeral KV/prefix caches, and persistent logs for that tenant are stored in the selected region. We do not route request payloads to other regions for processing unless explicitly configured in routing policies.

2) Retention windows: The default retention for request/response logs and model telemetry is 30 days. Customers may request shorter windows (e.g., 7 days) as part of their onboarding. We also offer a paid retention-extension option (90/365 days) for audit needs. See attached retention-sla-summary.pdf for the table and options.

3) Backups & replication: Regular backups are taken for control-plane metadata and deployment state. Customer request/response content is excluded from cross-region backups by default for region-locked tenants. For Dedicated or Private deployments we provide stronger guarantees; hosted customers can opt into dedicated snapshots (US-only) if needed.

I've attached: data-residency-faq.pdf (plain-language summary), retention-sla-summary.pdf (SLA table + purge timelines), and a redwood-soc2-evidence.zip stub containing pointers to our evidence artifacts.

Action items:
- If you want an auditable statement we can provide a one-page attestation that we will keep Acme data in US regions for your tenant — Ben, can you pick this up and route a doc (simple letter) to Gina?
- Marcus/Naomi: can you confirm the backup snapshot cadence and retention for hosted US-only tenants so I can call it out explicitly?

If helpful we can also schedule a 30-min call with InfoSec next week.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference

Attachments: data-residency-faq.pdf (PDF), retention-sla-summary.pdf (PDF), redwood-soc2-evidence.zip (ZIP)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Gina Park <gina@acmetech.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi_feldman@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 14:37:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API — residency & retention clarifications

Hi Gina — quick note from the sales side plus operational details Marcus provided: 

• Transient artifacts: The inference runtime uses ephemeral instance-local storage for short-lived caches (KV/prefix cache) which are zeroed on process termination. For US-region tenants we do not replicate instance-local ephemeral disks cross-region. Networked metrics/telemetry are aggregated to our metrics cluster in-region, then rolled up; raw request/response content is not used for metric aggregation.

• Deletion workflow: We have an automated retention worker that runs hourly to mark records past their retention window and a daily background job that purges marked records. Tombstones (small metadata records indicating a purge occurred) are retained for 7 days to support eventual consistency reconciliation only; they do not contain customer request/response payloads. Audit hooks: every purge run emits a signed audit event to the customer's audit log (stored in-region) and to our internal audit pipeline (SOC2-covered). See retention-sla-summary.pdf section 3 for the job cadence and retention of purge metadata.

• DPA clause: We can provide a DPA snippet specifying US-only processing and an obligation to disclose any cross-region transfers in advance; I'll attach a draft clause to the HubSpot deal and send you the Doc we discussed.

Next steps:
- I uploaded the attestation draft and the DPA snippet to the HubSpot deal and the Drive folder (links above).
- If Acme wants an on-record session, Marcus is available Wed/Thu for a 30-min technical deep-dive.

If you want me to convert the deletion workflow into a one-page diagram for your audit packet, say the word and we'll produce that today.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

- Add a 99.9% p95 latency SLO for request/response under 1k tokens (or equivalent latency banding)
- Reserve an additional 40% capacity for peak windows (Nov–Feb burst)

- Capacity: reserved burst will be carved from our Dedicated pool; we should discuss pre-warming windows and autoscale thresholds.

title: Billing cadence, net terms, and committed-discount options for Hosted API
preview:
From: Sarah Chen <sarah.chen@acme-corp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 28 Nov 2026 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: Hosted API billing cadence & net terms question

Hi Ben,

We're evaluating Redwood for a set of internal features and need clarification on billing cadence and payment terms before we move to procurement. Two quick questions: 1) Do you support monthly invoicing (purchase order -> invoice) for hosted API consumption, or is card-on-file required? 2) What net terms do you typically offer for annual/committed contracts (Net30/Net45/Net60)?

A few additional details: expected starting spend ~ $18k/month, likely ramp to $50k/month in 3–4 months. We'd prefer monthly invoices if possible to align with our internal GL process. Also please include a baseline rate card and any guidance on discounts for committed usage tiers.

Can you send the rate card and an example invoice flow (remittance fields, PO handling)? I've attached our PO template so you can confirm fields.

Thanks,
Sarah Chen
Director of Product
Acme Corporation
Attachments: acme_po_template.pdf (PDF, 45 KB)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Chen <sarah.chen@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 2026 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Hosted API billing cadence & net terms question

Hi Sarah — thanks for the details and the PO template. Short answers up front: yes, we support monthly invoicing (PO -> invoice) for hosted API customers, and we also accept card-on-file. Net terms are negotiable and depend on contract type and commit level — Net30 is standard for mid-market, Net45 can be approved by Finance for larger committed deals, and Net60 is rare but possible with legal/finance sign-off.

Attached: Redwood rate card (baseline hosted API), and a starter proposal showing a simple committed discount schedule. High-level options:
- Card-on-file (self-serve): fastest to onboard, no invoicing paperwork required.
- Monthly invoice (PO): supported; invoices issued monthly with line items for usage and any overage charges.
- Committed-usage discount: we can offer stepped discounts for 3–12 month committed spend. Typical anchor: 10–18% at ~$200k ARR equivalent, more for larger commitments.

Questions for your procurement team that will speed approvals:
1) Preferred net term target (Net30 vs Net45).
2) Billing contact and AP email for invoice routing.
3) Any required vendor forms or additional contract clauses.

If you like, I can loop in Lauren from Sales Ops to share standard invoice samples (with remittance info) and Karthik to run a quick committed-discount illustration based on your 3–6 month ramp.

Regards,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0183
Attachments: redwood_rate_card_v1.pdf (PDF), redwood-proposal-acme-v1.xlsx (Excel)


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Chen <sarah.chen@acme-corp.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 08:42:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Hosted API billing cadence & net terms question

Hi Sarah — looping in a couple quick implementation-friendly notes from Sales Ops:

- Invoice cadence: We produce consolidated invoices monthly (first of month for prior month usage). Each invoice includes: customer PO number, line items (usage, embeddings, reserved bursts if applicable), and remittance block.
- Net terms: We can support Net30 by default. For a committed 12-month agreement we can consider Net45 and an up-front partial prepay to secure a deeper discount.
- Discount path: For Hosted API we recommend a 6–12 month commit if you want a multi-percent discount band. Example: 6-month commit at $30k/mo -> 10% discount; 12-month at $25k/mo -> 15% discount + one-time onboarding credit. Exact structure depends on total ARR and payment cadence.

Operational asks if you prefer monthly invoicing:
1) AP contact + email for e-invoice routing
2) PO number on each invoice (we can accept a blanket PO + PO line item per month)
3) Confirm whether you need supplier forms (W-9, bank details) — we can provide these.

Ben — for ACME, given their stated ramp, I recommend we target: monthly invoicing, Net30, and offer a 6-month committed discount pilot (10%) with an onboarding credit of $1,000. Would you like me to draft that proposal?

Thanks,
Lauren Bishop
Senior Sales Operations Manager | Redwood Inference

title: Next steps — hosting choice & procurement readiness for Acme Health
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Claire Nguyen <claire@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 2026 14:02:00 -0800
Subject: Next steps — hosting choice & procurement readiness for Acme Health

Hi Claire — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned on procurement requirements and final hosting selection.

Recap (from our discovery)
- Primary need: secure, predictable inference for patient-facing summarization workflows (P0).
- Data classification: PHI-equivalent; must remain in VPC or dedicated tenancy.
- Timeline: procurement kickoff this month, contract signed by end of Q1 2027 if scope is stable.

Procurement/Commercial options we discussed
1) Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem): preferred for strict network controls and KMS integration; typically enterprise license + support.
2) Redwood Dedicated (reserved GPU capacity): good fit if they want predictable throughput without on-prem ops; supports commit-based pricing.
3) Redwood Hosted (on-demand): fastest to get started; good for pilots but less ideal for PHI unless we use private tenancy.

Simple asks for your procurement team (if you can confirm):
- Preferred vendor path: PO + vendor onboarding OR credit card + self-serve?
- Is Acme required to go through a marketplace listing (CloudMarketplace) for invoices/terms?
- Who owns redlines: Acme legal or third-party procurement? If you can share a preferred contract template that helps.

Pricing logistics to align before procurement kickoff
- If procurement prefers PO + revenue recognition via SAP/ERP, we should propose Dedicated with a 12-mo commit to simplify billing.
- If card/on-demand is required, we can pilot on Hosted for 60–90 days; recommend scoped commit post-pilot to drop unit price.

Attachments: I attached our latest SOC 2 summary and a draft Master Agreement + SOW template for your legal team to scan.

Proposed next steps
- Claire/procurement: confirm vendor route (PO vs card) and whether marketplace is required by Wed, Dec 3.
- Redwood: provide a 12-month Dedicated commit pricing and a Hosted pilot quote by Fri, Dec 6.
- Schedule a 30-min procurement sync next week to walk through onboarding and evidence requirements.

Thanks — happy to jump on a call with your procurement and IT security.

Best,
Camila Reyes

title: Premium assurance quote consolidation — uptime bands and escalation coverage
preview:
From: Samantha Li <samantha@elmhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Raj Patel <raj.patel@elmhealth.com>
Date: 2026-11-28T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Quick ask on SLA tiers + pricing

Ben —

Thanks again for the demo last week. Raj and I ran the team through latency and failover notes this morning; we like the model routing idea but need a clear pricing implication for production. Two quick asks so we can decide:

1) A simple mapping of uptime tiers to crediting (e.g., 99.9% -> X% credit, 99.95% -> Y%) and how that stacks for multi-region failover.
2) A banded estimate for annual committed spend that would unlock a 20% uplift in response coverage (onsite-hours / 24x7 escalation) and whether that discount is seat-based or consumption-based.

We attached our compliance checklist — not urgent but would be helpful if you can call out anything SLA-related.

We are pushing for a Q1 go-live and need the commercial guardrails to build the budget case for procurement. Looking for a quick bundle + price ranges by Monday if possible.

Thanks,
Samantha
VP Engineering
Elm Health

Attachments: Elm_Health_MAL-questions.pdf (pdf, 120KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Li <samantha@elmhealth.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Raj Patel <raj.patel@elmhealth.com>
Date: 2026-11-28T11:08:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Quick ask on SLA tiers + pricing

Samantha —

Appreciate the context and the compliance checklist. Quick answers up front, then I’ll sketch the pricing bands and attach a worksheet:

- Uptime crediting: our standard approach is linear slab credits for availability windows under a rolling 30-day measurement: 99.9% = 10% credit cap, 99.95% = 5% credit, 99.99% = 1% credit. Multi-region failover reduces credit impact if you have cross-region replication + active-active routing; I’ll note conditional adjustments in the spreadsheet.

- The 20% uplift in response coverage (i.e., move from business-hours triage to 24x7 escalation with named escalation roster) is typically tied to a minimum committed spend + retainer. We can do either:
  * Seat-based: per-named-oncall retainer + lower commit threshold (good if you want predictable seat costs)
  * Consumption-based: commit to token/throughput band for the year and we include the escalation overlay (better if volume is variable)

I’m attaching a pricing-worksheet-v3.xlsx with three illustrative bundles: Pilot, Production, and Enterprise. I’ll loop in Karthik (SE) to call out the ops delta and Laura (CS) to confirm SLA handoff and runbook expectations.

If you want, we can sketch a mutual action plan for the Q1 kickoff — timeline + gating items.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Mobile: +1 415-555-0112

Attachments: pricing-worksheet-v3.xlsx (xlsx, 48KB)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Li <samantha@elmhealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Raj Patel <raj.patel@elmhealth.com>
Date: 2026-11-29T08:34:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Quick ask on SLA tiers + pricing

Samantha / Ben —

Adding a technical lens to the commercial options Ben sent. Some practical calls you’ll want to make that affect price:

- Architecture for failover: active-active across two regions adds ~12–18% to baseline operational cost due to cross-region KV-sync and standby capacity. That reduces the size of the credit slab on availability incidents (i.e., you get smaller credits for outages because recovery is faster).

- Response coverage: 24x7 escalation with named on-call rotations includes a retainer that covers a triage layer + escalation to your SRE hours. Example: $6k/month retainer + surge hourly at $225/hr. Seat-based retainer is predictable; consumption retainer can be amortized into commit spend if you prefer.

- Example scenario (from the worksheet):
  * Annual commit $300k (consumption-based) -> we can offer 12% discount + included 24x7 escalation for up to 2 named oncall seats.

title: Billing anchor, minimum floors, and pilot concession for BrightMart
preview:
From: Jordan Li <jordan.li@brightmart.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-11-28T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Billing units for long-context workflows

Hi Camila,

Thanks for the demo yesterday — our engineering team has one outstanding question before we can move to procurement: 

- How does Redwood account tokens for hybrid calls where we send a 150k-character context (approx. 40k tokens) and then stream short completions? Specifically: do you round up per-call to the next 1k token increment, or do you charge exact tokens with a monthly minimum? Our infra team is worried about unexpectedly large bills if every long-context call gets rounded.

Also, can we get a short written example of how rounding/minimums would apply to a weekly batch of ~2,000 such calls (peak/avg patterns included)? If you have a sample invoice or workbook that shows the math that would help.

Jordan Li
Head of AI Engineering
BrightMart

Attachments: none

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Li <jordan.li@brightmart.com>
Date: 2026-11-28T13:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Billing units for long-context workflows

Jordan — thanks, great question. Short answer: we avoid per-call inflation by defaulting to exact token accounting at ingest + output, with an optional \"anchor floor\" model for committed customers to prevent variability. Details below and I attached a worked example workbook.

Key points:
- Token accounting: we charge for actual input tokens + output tokens. We tokenize at request time using the same tokenizer as the serving model (so you see identical numbers).
- Rounding: there is no system-level per-call 1k rounding for hosted pay-as-you-go. Rounding only applies when a contract has a minimum floor (e.g., monthly committed units) or for certain legacy pilot SKUs that still used quantum buckets.
- Anchor floor (what we propose for BrightMart): we set a monthly committed token floor (e.g., 2.5M tokens) and bill actual usage up to that floor. Above the floor you pay actual tokens. This prevents invoice spikes while still keeping unit economics explicit.

Worked example (see attached workbook):
- Weekly batch: 2k calls, each ~40k tokens input + 250 tokens avg output = 40,250 tokens/call -> 80.5M tokens/week.
- Without a commitment: you would be billed exact tokens for that week. No per-call rounding.
- With a 100M/month floor: your weekly usage would be credited against the floor; in this scenario, some weeks will use the whole floor and others will draw beyond it — smoothing happens at the monthly invoice level, not per-call.

Proposed pilot concession (to unblock procurement):
- 3-month pilot with a 50M/month anchor (floor) instead of a full-commit 100M. Any usage above the anchor is billed at list minus 10% pilot discount. At the end of pilot we reconvene on actuals and term.

I attached: proposed-commit-workbook.xlsx (breakdown by call, weekly/monthly rollups) and pricing_anchor_note.pdf (one-page explanation).

If helpful, I can loop in our billing operations lead (Tessa) and Rafael (sales lead) to approve the pilot parameters.

—Camila
Camila Reyes
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: proposed-commit-workbook.xlsx, pricing_anchor_note.pdf

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
To: Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Li <jordan.li@brightmart.com>
Date: 2026-11-29T10:20:00-08:00
Subject: FW: Billing units for long-context workflows — pilot concession ask

Tessa / Rafael — forwarding BrightMart's ask plus my recommended pilot concession above. TL;DR: they want a temporary lower anchor (50M/mo) for 3 months; we bill actuals above anchor at a 10% pilot discount. Workbook attached has weekly call examples and a sample invoice line. Can you confirm internal approval and any operational notes we need (reporting cadence, overage notification thresholds, invoice line item conventions)?

Excerpt from workbook (table snippet):
- Calls/week: 2,000
- Avg tokens/call: 40,250
- Tokens/week: 80,500,000
- Tokens/month (4.33 wks): ~348,665,000
- Anchor request: 50,000,000 tokens/month
- Expected overage (month 1): 298,665,000 tokens billed at pilot rate (list - 10%)

Operational asks for billing ops:
- Can billing show monthly reconciliation with line items: anchor credit (50M), billed tokens at list/pilot rate, and a short per-call sample row for audit?
- Are there anti-abuse floor protections we should add (to prevent synthetic token inflation through repeated long prefixes)? We don't want BrightMart to feel they need to throttle.

Thanks — once we have your ok I will send a short commercial term email for signature and route DocuSign.

—Camila

Attachments: proposed-commit-workbook.xlsx, pricing_anchor_note.pdf

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Li <jordan.li@brightmart.com>
Date: 2026-11-30T08:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: FW: Billing units for long-context workflows — pilot concession ask

Looks reasonable to me. Approving the 3-month 50M/month anchor with the overage at list -10% as a pilot concession. Tessa — please confirm we can produce the monthly reconciliation (anchor credit + per-call sample rows) and that we add the following protective language:
- We reserve the right to flag and review anomalous tokenization patterns indicative of synthetic inflation; any confirmed abuse will trigger an invoice adjustment.

If ops is good, Camila — proceed to send the short commercial term email and DocuSign link. I'll backfill any required sign-off internally.

—Rafael
VP, Sales, Redwood Inference

title: Mutual action plan: pilot milestones, ramping commits & true-up / price protection
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anna Reed <anna.reed@nimbus.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-11-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: MAP kickoff - pilot milestones, metrics, and commercial ask

Hi Anna —

Following our call this morning, attached is a first-pass mutual action plan (NIM-MAP-v1.pdf) that captures the evaluation milestones, acceptance criteria, and the proposed commercial ramp tied to milestone completion. High level:

- 30-day pilot (sandbox access) — success metrics: 95th pctl latency <120ms, token-efficiency baseline on the three test prompts we ran.
- 90-day ramp — target committed monthly usage: 150M tokens by month 3; discount ladder applied starting at 100M/month.
- Go/no-go gate at day 90 with acceptance signoff from your ML team.

Commercial asks I flagged for procurement: a pilot-support credit of $15k (applied to first invoice if we convert), and a graduated discount schedule tied to the committed ramp. We included a proposed true-up cadence (quarterly) and a 12-month price protection window from go-live in the attached SOW draft.

Could you review the MAP + SOW and highlight any procurement needs or definitions you want clarified (especially \"billable usage\" and what counts as credits/ingested data)? I know you said procurement will want explicit true-up mechanics and price-protection language.

If this looks reasonable, can we aim to lock the MAP and SOW by 11/15 so we can hit the 90-day gate before Q1 planning?

Thanks — happy to jump on quick call if helpful.

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com
+1 415-555-0182

Attachments: NIM-MAP-v1.pdf, SOW-pilot-draft.docx


From: Anna Reed <anna.reed@nimbus.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@nimbus.ai>
Date: 2026-11-02T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP kickoff - pilot milestones, metrics, and commercial ask

Ben —

Thanks for the MAP and SOW. High level this aligns with our timeline, but procurement pushed back on two items we need to address before legal will sign:

1) Price protection period — procurement prefers a 24-month floor, not 12. They want predictability if we lock-in a committed rate.
2) True-up mechanics — they want an annual true-up (not quarterly) with a clear calculation and caps on retroactive charges and an explicit lookback window.

Also, we need a precise definition for \"billable usage\": does it include pre-processing (tokenization) calls, embeddings for indexing, or only generation tokens? We need IDS on what triggers token consumption.

Can your legal and commercial team provide contract language that spells out: (a) true-up timing & cap, (b) price protection period and exceptions, and (c) unambiguous usage definitions? Procurement is OK moving forward on the pilot credit, but wants those items resolved for any committed discount ladder.

Best —
Anna Reed
Head of ML Operations | Nimbus Analytics
anna.reed@nimbus.ai
+1 212-555-4279


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anna Reed <anna.reed@nimbus.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-11-05T08:27:00-08:00
Subject: Re: MAP kickoff - pilot milestones, metrics, and commercial ask

Anna — thanks for the quick read. Appreciate the procurement perspective and that you flagged exact items. I pushed this to our commercial & legal team this morning and attached a short note with proposed redlines (redlines-price-protection.pdf). Summary of proposed compromises:

- Price protection: 18 months from go-live (middle ground). Exceptions for material cost increases (GPU spot pricing >25% sustained over 30 days) with 30-day notice.
- True-up cadence: quarterly true-ups but with a cap mechanism — retroactive charges limited to the greater of $10k or 5% of the quarter's invoice, and a 6-month lookback window. If you prefer annual true-ups, we can switch, but we need smaller caps to avoid large one-time bills; the quarterly cadence smooths billing volatility.
- Billable usage: defined as \"model inference tokens (input + output) consumed by calls to Redwood-hosted inference endpoints; embeddings counted separately under embeddings units; preprocessing (tokenization) and internal SDK calls are not billable unless they trigger inference requests.\" See section 3.a in the redline PDF.

If procurement is firm on 24 months we can escalate, but given the ramp profile I proposed 18 months to balance your predictability and our ability to offer deeper discounts for committed volume.

Attached: redlines-price-protection.pdf (highlights in-line), updated SOW with the ramp schedule annotated.

Would you be open to a brief (20m) call this week to work through the cap numbers? I can have Laura and Priyom join to speak to pricing mechanics and token accounting.

Best,
Ben

Attachments: redlines-price-protection.pdf, SOW-pilot-draft.docx


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-11-10T10:05:00-08:00
Subject: Fwd: MAP kickoff - procurement asks re: price protection / true-up — ask for guidance

Laura / Priyom —

Looping you in on the procurement feedback from Nimbus (thread below). Key asks: 24-month price protection or we propose 18 months; preferred annual true-up but procurement worried about lump-sum adjustments; definition of billable usage needs to be explicit.

Questions for commercial/legal:
- Are we comfortable with 18 months and the exception carveouts I proposed?
- The cap math (greater of $10k or 5% of quarter invoice) — is that acceptable from a revenue protection perspective?
- Any immediate redline language tweaks legal wants before I send back?

Priyom — can you confirm the model-run accounting for the three evaluation prompts so we can give procurement a worked example of how tokens map to billed units and what typical invoices would look like at 100M vs 150M/month?

Thanks — I want to keep momentum and lock the MAP by 11/15 if possible.

Ben


From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-11-12T14:18:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: MAP kickoff - procurement asks re: price protection / true-up — ask for guidance

Ben / Laura —

Quick update on token accounting: I ran the three evaluation prompts through our estimate harness. Ballpark numbers:
- Avg generation tokens per call (prompt+response): 1.4k tokens (varies by prompt).
- With 100M tokens/month that maps to ~71k inference calls; at 150M tokens/month ~107k calls.
- Embeddings usage is orthogonal — for your indexing job we'll estimate ~12M embedding tokens over the pilot unless they batch fingerprints.

On the commercial math: the 5% cap on retroactive charges is reasonable from a smoothing POV, but if Nimbus expects to self-report usage errors we should include a reconciliation mechanism and a dispute window (30 days) before any true-up is applied. Also recommend keeping quarterly true-ups but with an opt-in to annual true-up if both sides prefer to reduce accounting churn — that can be a checkbox in the SOW.

I'll draft a short example invoice table for procurement (100M vs 150M) and include it in the SOW as an appendix.

Priyom
Lead Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
priyom.das@redwoodinference.com


From: Anna Reed <anna.reed@nimbus.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@nimbus.ai>
Date: 2026-11-30T16:02:00-08:00
Subject: Re: MAP kickoff - pilot milestones, metrics, and commercial ask

Ben / Team —

Thanks — we reviewed your redlines and Priyom's invoice examples with the procurement team. We can accept the 18-month price protection as a compromise provided the GPU-cost exception is narrowly scoped to materially demonstrated cost shocks and requires 30 days' notice. We also prefer quarterly true-ups with the reconciliation/dispute window you proposed and the retroactive cap at the greater of $10k or 5% per quarter.

Procurement asked for the acceptance workflow: once you incorporate the redlined language into the SOW and send a DocuSign link, procurement will run a final check and aim to sign within 5 business days. We will plan the pilot start for 12/08 contingent on the SOW being signed by 12/04.

Please send the updated SOW + DocuSign link and we'll route it to Legal/Procurement.

Anna
Head of ML Operations | Nimbus Analytics
anna.reed@nimbus.ai
+1 212-555-4279

title: Renewal: reserved capacity increase to 32 GPUs — pricing & SLA proposal
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Julia Park <julia@ataplan.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 28 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Renewal: reserved capacity increase to 32 GPUs — pricing & SLA proposal

Hi Julia,

Thanks again for the conversation yesterday. As discussed, Ataplan's current Dedicated pool (16 GPUs) is up for renewal on 2027-01-15. Given the Q1 product ramp you described, attached is a straightforward renewal + expansion proposal to move to a 32-GPU reserved pool with a production throughput guarantee.

Two paths on the table (attached workbook for blended math):

1) Immediate 32-GPU reserved commitment (12 months) — simplest operationally.
   - Per-GPU reserved rate (hourly): $1.35/hr
   - Throughput guarantee: sustained pool throughput of 300k tokens/sec (aggregate) with a 99th percentile tail latency target we can iterate on with your SREs.
