title: Re: CPRA addendum — telemetry handling & subprocessor onboarding
preview:
From: Claire Morris <claire.morris@big-retail.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Legal Team <legal@big-retail.com>, procurement@big-retail.com
Date: Mon, 23 Nov 2026 10:12:00 -0800
Subject: CPRA addendum — telemetry + subprocessors

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the demo last week — Big Retail is moving fast on the pilot. Our privacy team has reviewed Redwood's DPA and asked for a short CPRA addendum to explicitly confirm:

- Redwood's classification as a \"service provider\" under CPRA/CPRA;
- How telemetry (system logs, usage metrics, aggregated analytics) is handled and retained;
- The process and timing for adding new subprocessors and how we receive notice; and
- Whether any telemetry could be used to re-identify customer end users.

I've attached a draft addendum with suggested redlines. Can you confirm who on your side will take point and whether Redwood can accept the short notice period we requested (10 business days) for new subprocessors?

Claire Morris
Senior Counsel — Privacy
Big Retail Co

Attachment: CPRA_Addendum_Draft_v1.docx

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
To: Claire Morris <claire.morris@big-retail.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 23 Nov 2026 13:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: CPRA addendum — telemetry + subprocessors

Claire —

Appreciate the clear summary and the draft. Short answers up front: we are comfortable with the \"service provider\" classification in the CPRA context; telemetry is collected for operational purposes and is not sold or used to build new products for other customers; and we can commit to a subprocessors notice cadence, but the notice period is something we'd like to align on.

A few specifics we'll propose in our redline:
- Telemetry scope: \"system logs, aggregated usage metrics, latency and error telemetry, operational traces; excludes PII & content of customer-managed data flows unless expressly consented.\"
- Retention: default 90 days for most telemetry; 365 days for security logs (audit, incident investigations) unless otherwise requested in an enterprise configuration.
- Subprocessor onboarding: we publish a current list and provide 15 business days' notice before onboarding a new subprocessor with an opt-out route (customer can request that data routing be restricted) — we can be flexible for enterprise contracts.

I've attached our proposed redline and the current subprocessor list. Ben will follow up on telemetry retention examples. Let us know the privacy team's must-haves.

Thanks,
Aisha Rahman
Head of Sales — Redwood Inference
(aisha.rahman@redwood.com)

Attachments: CPRA_Addendum_Draft_v1.docx (redline), Subprocessor_List_202611.pdf

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Claire Morris <claire.morris@big-retail.com>; Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Nov 2026 09:20:00 -0800
Subject: Re: CPRA addendum — telemetry + subprocessors

Claire — quick technical notes on telemetry and re-identification risk:

- Telemetry types: we capture request metadata (timestamps, model id, token counts), server-side resource metrics (GPU/CPU util, queue depth), and anonymized latency/error traces. We do not store customer prompt/content as telemetry.
- Re-identification: telemetry is aggregated and sampled by default. We do not attempt to correlate telemetry with end-user identities; that practice would be inconsistent with our service-provider commitments.
- Retention: 90 days for aggregated metrics; 365 days for security/audit logs. For regulated pilots we can offer custom retention (e.g., 30 days) and scope isolation under Redwood Private deployments.
- Subprocessor notice: our standard is 15 business days. For cases where a subprocessor change is critical to the customer's data flow we can provide earlier notice, and we accept a formal objection process during the notice window.

If helpful I can draft the exact redline language referencing CPRA \"service provider\" obligations and an objection process.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Sr. Solutions Engineer — Redwood Inference



From: Claire Morris <claire.morris@big-retail.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Legal Team <legal@big-retail.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Nov 2026 14:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: CPRA addendum — telemetry + subprocessors

Thanks Aisha/Ben — this is helpful. Two follow-ups from privacy:

1) Our counsel is pushing back on the 15 business day notice; they'd prefer 10. Is Redwood able to commit to 10 in a narrow set of cases (pilot + low-risk subprocessors)? If not, what alternative mitigations can you offer (e.g., restriction on data routing until customer signs off, or allowing us to require certain subprocessors to be removed from our tenant)?

2) Can you confirm whether telemetry could contain any hashes or identifiers derived from customer content (e.g., prompt fingerprints)? They flagged a scenario where hashes could be used together with other data to re-identify.

We can accept 15 days as a fallback but need a vendor response for our procurement timeline.

Claire



From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Claire Morris <claire.morris@big-retail.com>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 25 Nov 2026 16:47:00 -0800
Subject: Re: CPRA addendum — telemetry + subprocessors

Claire —

Appreciate the specificity. Quick answers then a proposed path forward:

- Notice window: we can accept a 10-business-day notice for subprocessors that are considered low-risk (CDNs, observability providers that operate on redacted telemetry) and for pilots where there is explicit scope limitation. For subprocessors that will process content or PII, we need 15 business days and the ability to map data flows as part of onboarding.

- Telemetry fingerprints/hashes: we do not persist customer-derived content hashes as part of standard telemetry. If a customer elects to enable additional debug traces for troubleshooting, those are captured only with customer opt-in and labeled as diagnostics; they are stored under stricter retention and access controls. We'll add explicit language to the addendum confirming no storage of customer-derived identifiers in telemetry by default.

Proposed next steps:
1) I'll circulate a narrow redline to clause 3 (Service Provider obligations) and clause 7 (Subprocessor onboarding) that codifies: 10-business-day notice for low-risk subprocessors; 15 for others; an objection process; and an explicit telemetry non-reidentification statement.
2) Aisha/Ben — can you confirm acceptance so we can get procurement sign-off on Big Retail's timeline?

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Director, Privacy & Compliance Programs — Redwood Inference

Attachment: CPRA_Addendum_Draft_v1.docx (redline v2)

title: Clarify subprocessor evidence access & notification timing
preview:
From: Nate Wu <nate.wu@horizonpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: HorizonPay Security <security@horizonpay.com>
Date: Mon, 23 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Request: evidence access & notification timing for incident HPAY-2026-11

Hi Ben,

We have an active incident on our side that appears to involve traffic passing through Redwood's inference nodes (HPAY-2026-11). Our security team needs to confirm whether a subset of customer identifiers appear in recent token logs and to understand the timeline for notification under our DPA. Can you confirm: 1) Redwood's contractual notification window for this type of event, and 2) the process and expected turnaround to obtain a filtered set of token logs (pseudonymized) or a forensics snapshot from any subprocessors involved?

I've attached a small redacted sample and a short timeline of our incident. At minimum we need an initial acknowledgement and whether PII is reasonably likely to have been exposed. Our asks are:
- Initial acknowledgment + triage note within 48 hours
- Ability to receive a pseudonymized log slice or proof of non-exposure within 10 business days

Can you point me to the right contact for a secure upload (SFTP / transfer link) and confirm any required legal steps (ROE, signed addendum)?

Thanks,
Nate
Nate Wu
Head of Security Operations, HorizonPay
Attachment: forensic_sample_logs_redacted.tar.gz (log archive, 4MB)



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Nate Wu <nate.wu@horizonpay.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 23 Nov 2026 11:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: evidence access & notification timing for incident HPAY-2026-11

Hi Nate — thanks for the heads-up and the sample. We're taking this seriously and want to keep things narrow and fast. A few quick points from Redwood's side:

1) Contractual window: our standard DPA language sets a 72-hour "reasonable efforts" notification for confirmed incidents that meet the DPA definition of a personal data breach. We can provide an earlier acknowledgement (24 hours) and a triage note where feasible, but "confirmation" may take longer depending on the scope.

2) Subprocessor access & evidence: we can produce a pseudonymized log slice or an attestational forensic snapshot. For operational and security reasons we require:
   - Signed ROE (rules of engagement) + NDA/addendum for forensic access
   - A narrowly scoped selector (time window, affected tenant ids, log types) — we cannot provide broad raw token streams
   - Transfer via our secure upload (pre-signed S3 link or SFTP)

3) Timelines we can commit to (proposed):
   - Initial acknowledgement & assigned liaison: within 24 hours of this email
   - Triage note (high-level exposure assessment): within 72 hours
   - Delivery of pseudonymized log slice / attestational snapshot: target 7-10 business days after ROE is accepted and selectors finalized

4) Subprocessor involvement: if a subcontracted infra provider holds the requested artifacts, we'll coordinate and either provide the data ourselves or (where the subproc permits) request the subproc to upload directly; this can add a negotiation step.

I'm attaching a pointer to our SOC2 and the standard evidence request template to speed review.

Attachments: redwood_soc2_report_q3_2026.pdf, evidence_request_template.docx (template)

Ben
Ben Carter
Director, Security Partnerships | Redwood Inference

title: Latency budgeting and context-window tradeoffs — post-demo follow-up
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Chris Nolan <chris.nolan@apexhealth.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 24 Nov 2026 15:10:00 -0800
Subject: Latency budgeting and context-window tradeoffs — post-demo follow-up

Hi Chris —

Thanks again for taking the demo earlier. Quick recap and a few clarifying questions so we can scope the performance validation correctly: 

Summary from the demo
- You ran the clinical summarization workflow over patient notes and want streaming partial outputs into the UI (token-by-token or chunked lines).
- You estimated peak concurrent users ~200, typical concurrent active sessions ~60.
- Typical request size: concat of patient history + encounter notes ~60k characters (~9–12k tokens), output summary target 600–1,200 tokens.
- Target p95 latency for perceived interactivity in the UI: <= 250 ms for first partial token and <= 700 ms for full response completion under steady concurrency.

Open questions (so our benchmark matches production):
1) Streaming granularity — do you need single-token streaming (low-latency partial token updates) or can you accept 256-token chunked flushes?
2) Concurrency profile — is the 200 peak primarily simultaneous live inference calls, or do many requests batch (e.g., background summarization jobs)?
3) Max context requirement — is the 9–12k token input a hard maximum or are there cases reaching ~32k tokens?
4) Tolerance for model/quantization tradeoffs — would you accept a slightly lower-quality 16-bit-quantized variant if it cuts p95 by ~30%?

Next steps I propose:
- If you can send a small sample of representative requests (anonymized) and confirm streaming granularity, we’ll run a 3-tier benchmark (hosted small-scale, Redwood Dedicated sim, and fallback hosted with batching) and share detailed token-latency curves.
- Internal prep: I’m looping Marissa (solutions) and Rishi (inference lead) to propose hardware and config.

Attachments: redwood-performance-plan.xlsx (initial benchmark matrix), apex-sample-queries.json (placeholder)

Would you be able to: send a short anonymized sample (5–10 requests) and confirm streaming granularity by end of week?

Thanks,
Ben
Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference


From: Chris Nolan <chris.nolan@apexhealth.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 24 Nov 2026 17:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Latency budgeting and context-window tradeoffs — post-demo follow-up

Ben —

Great recap, thanks. Answers inline below.

Streaming granularity
- We need near real-time feedback in the UI. Single-token streaming is preferred for clinicians watching summary appear live; if that is infeasible, 128–256 token chunking is our second preference.

Concurrency profile
- 200 peak is for interactive users during morning rounds. Background batch summarization is separate (runs overnight) and can be lower priority. So please assume 200 simultaneous interactive inference calls at peak.

Max context
- The 9–12k token range covers 95% of cases. We have a small subset (~1–2% of encounters) with extremely long histories that can reach ~28k tokens. For those we’d accept a hybrid approach (server-side truncation + context-slicing + fetching additional context on demand).

Quality tolerance
- We prefer preserving clinical fidelity. Avoid any configuration that risks hallucinations in summaries; small quantization tradeoffs are acceptable if we can validate outputs on our clinical testset.

Procurement questions (cc'd procurement): what are the cost differentials between hosted vs Redwood Dedicated for steady 200 concurrent interactive users with streaming? Also need SLA for p95 latency and data residency assurances.

I’ll send an anonymized 10-sample bundle by EOD tomorrow.

—Chris
Head of Clinical AI | ApexHealth


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 25 Nov 2026 08:30:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Latency budgeting and context-window tradeoffs — post-demo follow-up

Marissa / Rishi — forwarding Chris's answers from ApexHealth. Key requirements: single-token preferred streaming, 200 interactive concurrency peak, 9–12k tokens common with 1–2% up to ~28k, strict quality (clinical). Procurement is asking hosted vs Dedicated cost/SLA delta.

Action items I’d like from the two of you: 
- Marissa: can you draft a short benchmark scope and list of artifacts we need from the customer to run a realistic sim (sample requests, concurrency ramp profile, pass/fail criteria on clinical QA)? Put estimated calendar and who owns the run.
- Rishi: based on preliminary numbers, what hardware+config would you recommend to achieve first-token p95 <=250 ms and end-to-end <=700 ms for 12k token inputs with single-token streaming at 200 concurrency? If full 28k worst-cases are too expensive to meet the same SLO, propose a hybrid pattern (truncate+fetch or fallbacks) and rough cost delta.

I’ll respond to procurement with a high-level hosted vs Dedicated comparison once we have your inputs.

Thanks,
Ben


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 25 Nov 2026 11:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Fwd: Latency budgeting and context-window tradeoffs — post-demo follow-up

Thanks Ben — taking this on. Quick notes and ask list so we can scope the benchmark: 

Benchmark scope (high level)
- Three phases: baseline (hosted API, single replica), Dedicated mimic (reserved capacity with 2 config variants), and degradation tests (simulate 28k outliers + fallback pattern).
- Metrics: p50/p95 first-token, p50/p95 completion, tokens/sec throughput, error/hallucination rate on an agreed clinical testset, memory/kv-cache footprint, and cost per 1k requests.

Artifacts we need from ApexHealth
- 10 anonymized representative requests (they said they'll send 10) + 5 long-outlier examples (~25–28k tokens).
- Concurrency profile (ramp pattern: e.g., 0->200 in 2 minutes, sustained 20 minutes, spike patterns).
- Clinical QA rubric (what counts as acceptable vs regression).

Hardware/config ask (Rishi — preliminary recommendation)
- For single-token streaming at 200 concurrency with 12k token inputs: start with Dedicated class: 4xA100-80GB equivalent GPUs behind a Redwood routing pool (sharding KV cache and continuous batching disabled for streaming critical paths). Expect first-token p95 in the 180–280 ms range depending on model variant; full completion p95 will vary with output length and may be 500–900 ms.
- For hosted fallbacks: recommend an autoscaled pool with lower-cost quantized models for non-critical summaries and a hot path on pinned Dedicated for interactive sessions.

Risk & fallback patterns
- Long-context (>16k) increases token-processing overhead — preference: server-side context slicing with retrieval + progressive summarization; that keeps interactive SLOs while preserving fidelity.

Deliverables and timeline
- I’ll produce a benchmark plan doc and a runbook (ETA 48 hours). Rishi — if you can confirm the cluster sizing and a rough cost delta (hosted vs Dedicated for 200 concurrency) by EOD tomorrow, we’ll send this to Apex and schedule a 2-week POC window.

Attachments: updated benchmark matrix (I’ll drop into the sheet)

Marissa
Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference

title: Geo boundary snapshot and KMS routing query
preview:
From: Anita Shah <anita.shah@medscope.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: MedScope Security Team <security@medscope.com>
Date: 2026-11-25 14:12:00 +0000
Subject: Request: regional hosting snapshot + KMS routing details for onboarding

Hi Ben,

Hope you’re well — we’re moving forward with the pilot and need a short package to complete our internal SIG/CAIQ review and legal onboarding. Specifically can you provide the following for EU production: 

- A per-region snapshot that shows where customer data may be processed/stored (nodes/zones) — we need the minimal set of locations and any standby failover zones.
- A KMS routing map that indicates which keys are stored/managed in which jurisdiction and whether keys are customer-managed or Redwood-managed.
- Confirmation of VPC/privatelink behaviour: which egress paths are used for telemetry/metrics, and whether any control-plane callbacks cross regions.
- Any runbook/excerpt describing how we failover between regions without crossing EU borders for persistent data (short bullet list is fine).

Target deadline: Friday COB (3 business days). If you can’t meet that exactly, a staged delivery (snapshot + KMS map today, runbook next) is OK.

Also include filenames for anything you attach. We’ll reference these in our SIG evidence pack.

Thanks,
Anita
Anita Shah
Security Operations Manager, MedScope
anita.shah@medscope.com

[attachment: none]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Anita Shah <anita.shah@medscope.com>
Cc: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
